1: "You are the children of the LORD your God." Identity precedes obligation — sonship is established before any prohibition is addressed. The dietary and mourning laws that follow are grounded in who Israel already is, not in what they must do to become acceptable. The NT develops this sequence explicitly in Galatians 4:4–6: "God sent his Son... so that we might receive adoption as sons. And because you are sons, God has sent the Spirit of his Son into our hearts." In Christ, the sonship announced to Israel is extended to all who are united to him. Theme.
2: "You are a people holy to the LORD your God, and the LORD has chosen you to be his treasured possession." The double identity: qāḏôš (holy) and sĕgullâ (treasured possession). Peter applies both directly to the church in Christ: "you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession" (1 Peter 2:9 — the same ʿam sĕgullâ language). The election-identity of Israel becomes the election-identity of the church through union with Christ. Direct connection.
3: "You shall not eat any detestable thing." The prohibition of what is toʿēḇâ — an abomination — marks the boundary of covenant identity. The dietary laws as a whole create a visible distinction between Israel and the surrounding nations. As a shadow: the physical distinctions that maintained Israel's separation from the nations are superseded when Christ's mission expands to all nations. Acts 10:15 — "What God has made clean, do not call common" — is the declaration that the separating wall has served its purpose. Shadow.
4: Clean land animals permitted: ox, sheep, and goat — the primary domestic livestock, the animals of the covenant sacrificial system. The clean animals are precisely those used in Israel's offerings. As a revelation of God's design: the animals that sustain daily life and the animals that serve covenant worship overlap — the sacred and the everyday are not compartmentalized. In Christ, this integration is complete: "whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God" (1 Corinthians 10:31). Revelation of God.
5: Wild game permitted: deer, gazelle, roe deer, wild goat, ibex, antelope, mountain sheep. The extension of clean status to wild animals not used in the sacrificial system — edible food is not limited to the sanctuary's animals. The goodness of creation extends beyond the cultic system. In Christ, the goodness of all creation is affirmed (1 Timothy 4:4 — "everything created by God is good, and nothing is to be rejected if it is received with thanksgiving"). Revelation of God.
6: The two identifying marks of clean land animals: completely split hooves and chewing the cud. The visible taxonomy creates a system of distinction perceivable to any observer. The physical markers of cleanness point toward the inner transformation the covenant truly requires — a correspondence between the visible and the real. What the external law can only mark visibly, Christ produces inwardly: the Spirit-transformed heart that matches the outer confession (Romans 2:29; 2 Corinthians 3:3). Theme.
7: Animals that chew the cud but lack split hooves (camel, hare, rock badger) are unclean — one mark is not enough; both are required. The strictness of the double-criterion points to the completeness of what God requires. The clean animal must fully satisfy both conditions. Christ fully satisfies every covenantal requirement without partial compliance — "he fulfilled all righteousness" (Matthew 3:15) and "has done everything well" (Mark 7:37). Theme.
8: The pig — split hooves but does not chew the cud — is the paradigmatic unclean animal. Jesus uses pig-related imagery in the parable of the Prodigal Son (Luke 15:15–16): the son's lowest degradation is feeding pigs, longing for their food. The pig represents both ritual uncleanness and creaturely existence at its most debased. The Father's reception of the son who was among pigs is the gospel of radical restoration: no level of uncleanness places a person beyond the Father's embrace. Theme.
9: Water creatures with fins and scales are clean. The visible structures of the fish — fins to move, scales to protect — are the markers of ordered, purposeful life. As a theme of God's design: the Creator marks out what sustains life and what does not. In Christ, the Creator who designed the distinctions between clean and unclean comes in person — "the Word through whom all things were made" (John 1:3) is the same one who transcends these distinctions in himself, declaring all foods clean (Mark 7:18–19). Revelation of God.
10: Whatever lacks fins or scales is unclean. The principle: the absence of the ordering structures makes the creature unusable for the covenant community. As a shadow: the clean/unclean system prepares for the day when Christ, by his atonement, removes the barrier of uncleanness and makes all peoples accessible to the covenant community. "What God has made clean, do not call common" (Acts 10:15) — the vision that ends the dietary separation is the vision that opens the Gentile mission. Shadow.
11: "You may eat any clean bird." The general permission for clean birds. The Deuteronomic code assumes the daily life of God's people — eating is a covenantal act. In Christ, all eating becomes eucharistic in principle: "So whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God" (1 Corinthians 10:31). The clean-bird permission is one facet of the covenant structure that integrates worship and daily life. Revelation of God.
12: The eagle, bearded vulture, and black vulture are unclean — birds of prey and carrion. These birds are associated with death (scavenging carcasses) and violence (predation). The unclean birds are those most associated with death and carrion. In Christ, death itself is defeated: the birds that feed on the dead are denied their feast because the one they would feed on rises. Revelation 19:17–21, which describes birds feasting on the carcasses of God's enemies at the final judgment, is the eschatological inversion. Theme.
13: Kite and every kind of falcon — unclean birds of prey. The list continues with birds of prey. As a theme: the prohibition on birds that take life by force reflects the covenant community's alignment with the God of life rather than of predatory violence. Jesus' instructions to his disciples inverse the predatory-force model: "I am sending you out as sheep in the midst of wolves" (Matthew 10:16) — the covenant community embodies non-predatory vulnerability, trusting the God of life. Theme.
14: Every kind of raven — unclean. The raven is significant in the OT: Noah sends the raven first (Genesis 8:7); ravens feed Elijah at the Wadi Cherith (1 Kings 17:4). Even the unclean raven is fed by God and used by God as a servant. Jesus uses ravens to illustrate God's providential care: "Consider the ravens: they neither sow nor reap... yet God feeds them. Of how much more value are you than the birds!" (Luke 12:24). The raven, though ritually unclean, testifies to the Father's universal providence. Theme.
15: Ostrich, nighthawk, sea gull, hawk — unclean birds. The list moves through the spectrum of birds associated with the wilderness, night, and sea. As a revelation of God's order: every creature exists within a system the Creator designed. The unclean animals are not chaotic but ordered — placed in their categories by the same wisdom that orders all creation. In Christ, "the wisdom of God" (1 Corinthians 1:24) is the creative ordering principle made visible. Revelation of God.
16: Little owl, screech owl, barn owl — unclean. Owls are nocturnal, associated with the dark. The light-dark contrast runs through biblical theology: YHWH creates light and separates it from darkness (Genesis 1:3–4). In Christ: "I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life" (John 8:12). The creatures of darkness are unclean; the people of the covenant belong to the light. Theme.
17: Pelican, carrion vulture, cormorant — unclean. These are water birds associated with death-feeding or fish-taking. Even in this administrative list of prohibited birds, the underlying principle is the Creator's ordering of creation — the covenant people eat within a designed system that reflects the wisdom of their God. All such ordering finds its source in the Word through whom all things were created (John 1:3; Colossians 1:16). Revelation of God.
18: Stork, heron, hoopoe, bat — unclean. The bat is technically a mammal but is grouped with the birds. The list covers the full range of flying creatures associated with scavenging, night, and wetlands. As a broader theme: the clean/unclean system in its entirety anticipates the day when Christ declares the system fulfilled and the mission expands to all peoples. "What God has made clean, do not call common" (Acts 10:15) — the list of unclean birds is overturned in the declaration that opens the Gentile mission. Shadow.
19: All winged insects are unclean — they shall not be eaten. The general prohibition covers the final category of flying creatures. (Leviticus 11:20–23 provides the exception for locusts, which John the Baptist eats — Mark 1:6. The prophetic figure in the wilderness lives by the boundary-edge of the law.) As a theme: the comprehensive taxonomic system prepares for the comprehensive fulfillment in Christ, who cleanses not a category at a time but the whole order by his atoning work. Theme.
20: "But all clean birds you may eat." The permissive summary. The covenant God is a God of permission and provision, not only prohibition. The gifts of creation are for the enjoyment of the covenant community. In Christ, the goodness of creation is affirmed and extended: "everything created by God is good, and nothing is to be rejected if it is received with thanksgiving, for it is made holy by the word of God and prayer" (1 Timothy 4:4–5). Revelation of God.
21: Israel shall not eat anything that dies of itself; the prohibition closes with: "You shall not boil a young goat in its mother's milk." The life-perverting prohibition: the milk designed to nourish and sustain the kid must not become the medium of its death. The principle: God's life-giving provisions must not be perverted into instruments of death. In Christ, the perversion of life-giving creation is reversed — what was given for life but became the instrument of death (the covenant law itself, through human sin) is now restored to its life-giving purpose (Romans 7:10–12; Deuteronomy 8:1–4). Theme.
22: "You shall tithe all the yield of your seed that comes from the field each year." The tithe as the systematic acknowledgment that every harvest belongs to YHWH — the first tenth is the representative portion that consecrates the whole. In Christ, the principle of first-fruits offering is fulfilled and reframed: "Each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver" (2 Corinthians 9:7). The tithe becomes freewill generosity; the form changes, the principle — all belongs to God — remains. Theme.
23: Eat the tithe before the LORD at the central sanctuary "so that you may learn to fear the LORD your God always." The tithe feast before YHWH is a formative covenant meal — eating in God's presence cultivates reverence. The Lord's Supper is the NT's covenant meal before the Lord — not for the formation of fear but of gratitude, remembrance, and union: "For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes" (1 Corinthians 11:26). The feast in YHWH's presence becomes the feast in Christ's presence. Theme.
24: If the journey to the sanctuary is too long, convert the tithe to money. The covenant's practical accommodation to distance — the principle of tithing is preserved even when the original form is impossible. The NT extends this pragmatic flexibility in maintaining the giving principle: Paul's collection for the Jerusalem church (2 Cor 8–9) is the covenant community caring for one another across geographic distances, the tithing principle operating through new channels. Theme.
25: Convert the tithe to money and take it to the place the LORD will choose. The flexibility of the covenant's economic provisions reflects the God who works within human conditions. In Christ, the entire economy of covenant giving is transformed from obligation to overflow: "we have received grace upon grace" (John 1:16); "give, and it will be given to you" (Luke 6:38). The tithe-to-money conversion is a small sign of the covenant's ongoing adaptation to life's realities. Revelation of God.
26: "Spend the money for whatever you desire... and eat there before the LORD your God and rejoice, you and your household." The tithe feast is genuinely festive — the worshipper chooses freely and rejoices. The tithe is not joyless obligation; it culminates in a celebration before YHWH that includes the worshipper's own pleasure. In Christ, the feast-motif culminates in the eschatological wedding banquet: "Blessed are those who are invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb" (Revelation 19:9). The tithe feast's joy is a foretaste of the ultimate celebration. Theme.
27: "Do not neglect the Levite who lives in your towns, for he has no portion or inheritance among you." The Levite — landless by divine design, sustained by covenant provision — must not be forgotten in the celebration. The principle of caring for those whose calling precludes conventional income continues in the NT: "Let the elders who rule well be considered worthy of double honor, especially those who labor in preaching and teaching" (1 Timothy 5:17). The pattern of sustaining those who minister the covenant community is rooted in the Levitical system. Theme.
28: Every third year the tithe stays in the local towns for distribution. The triennial tithe as a systematic local welfare mechanism — not brought to the central sanctuary but kept nearby for the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow. The early church's daily distribution of food (Acts 6:1), care for widows (1 Timothy 5:3–16), and Paul's collection for the poor (Romans 15:26–27) are the NT expressions of this covenant welfare principle. Theme.
29: "So that the Levite... and the resident foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow... may come and eat and be satisfied. Then the LORD your God will bless you in all the work of your hands." Generosity to the vulnerable brings YHWH's blessing. Jesus' teaching that care for "the least of these" is service to him (Matthew 25:40) and the early church's communal care echo this covenant mechanism. In Christ, the blessing that follows covenant generosity has its ultimate source: "He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?" (Romans 8:32). Revelation of God.
